_description: Tier 1 scenario family weights. Maps a MarketState (carry regime, target distance, tenor, vol) to a weight per scenario family. Weights are then applied to per-family mean P&L to produce a single weighted P&L score per structure. Same architecture as affinity_scores.json: baseline + scaled adjustments, normalised, with a floor.
_what_the_weights_mean: Each weight expresses how much we believe a given scenario family represents what the market is likely to do — given the trade view and the current market state. They are NOT structure-dependent: the same vector is applied to every shortlisted structure so that resulting scores are directly comparable.
_baseline_logic: All 8 families start at 1/8 = 0.125 (equal-weighted, no view). Contexts nudge individual families up or down based on conditions evaluated against MarketState. After all contexts fire, weights are floored (no family below `floor`) and renormalised to sum to 1.

_context_schema.id: Stable identifier — surfaced in the UI so the PM can see which contexts are active for this trade.
_context_schema.comment: Plain-English description of what this market state means and why the weights move as they do. This is the reasoning the PM reads when sense-checking.
_context_schema.when: Array of {field, op, value} — ALL must be true for this context to apply. Supported fields: target_z_abs, carry_regime, with_carry, T, vol, atmfsratio. Supported ops: '>', '>=', '<', '<=', '==', '!='. If a referenced field is None on MarketState (e.g. target_z when no target supplied), the condition evaluates false and the context does not fire.
_context_schema.adjustments: Object mapping family name → signed delta. All families listed here are adjusted simultaneously when the context fires. Positive = increase weight (this scenario matters more), negative = decrease (matters less).

contexts.comment: Target is far from forward (>1.5σ). Reaching it requires a sustained, conviction-driven move — which typically overshoots. Fat-tail outcomes deserve more weight. Conversely, the chance of touching the level early and reversing is low at this distance.

Target is close to the forward (<0.5σ) — easily reachable on a normal random walk. The market is likely to oscillate around it; early touches and pull-backs are the dominant risk for capped structures. Overshooting a level this close is less informative.

High carry (regime 2), trading with the carry: drift is your friend. The forward is already pricing a smooth move toward the target. CORRECT_PATH is the most likely realised outcome; wrong-way scenarios require swimming against both drift and realised carry.

High carry (regime 2), trading against the carry: the carry is a constant structural headwind. The most likely path is adverse drift, not the target. A flat market (no-move) is also bad because carry bleeds premium while the trade goes nowhere. The PM needs to stare at WRONG_WAY and NO_MOVE.

Carry regime 0 = carry signal is weak relative to vol. With no clear drift, the market is more likely to be driven by vol mean-reversion than directional carry. NO_MOVE is less meaningful here (carry doesn't make flat bad), and vol-sensitive outcomes deserve more attention.

Tenor < 1 month: theta and pin-risk dominate the trade. EXPIRY (intrinsic value at maturity) is what matters most because there is no time for mid-life path scenarios to differ meaningfully from the terminal outcome. EARLY_TARGET becomes nearly equivalent to EXPIRY at this horizon.

Tenor > 6 months: plenty of time for spot to wander to the target and then drift away before expiry. EARLY_TARGET (touching the level, then reversing) is a real structural risk for capped or spread structures over this horizon.

ATM vol > 20%: large vol moves are a material source of P&L variation beyond directional outcome alone. Vega-rich structures (long gamma, long vol) benefit; short-vol structures are exposed. VOL_SENSITIVITY scenarios deserve more weight to surface this.
